title: StereoSet: Measuring stereotypical bias in pretrained language models
authors: Moin Nadeem; Anna Bethke; Siva Reddy
short_summary: Introduces a large-scale benchmark for stereotypical associations in language models across gender, profession, race, and religion.
research_relevance: Useful as an early stereotype-measurement template; can inspire migration/nationality stereotype item design.

title: CrowS-Pairs: A Challenge Dataset for Measuring Social Biases in Masked Language Models
authors: Nikita Nangia; Clara Vania; Rasika Bhalerao; Samuel R. Bowman
short_summary: Pairs minimally different sentences where one reflects a stereotype and the other does not, then tests model preference.
research_relevance: Good template for pairwise migrant/non-migrant or nationality-framed stereotype tests.

title: BBQ: A hand-built bias benchmark for question answering
authors: Alicia Parrish; Angelica Chen; Nikita Nangia; Vishakh Padmakumar; Jason Phang; Jana Thompson; Phu Mon Htut; Samuel Bowman
short_summary: Tests whether QA models rely on social stereotypes under ambiguous contexts and whether bias persists when context is informative.
research_relevance: Highly relevant because its ambiguity design can be adapted to immigration, nationality, refugee status, or legal decision settings.

title: BOLD: Dataset and Metrics for Measuring Biases in Open-Ended Language Generation
authors: Jwala Dhamala; Tony Sun; Varun Kumar; Satyapriya Krishna; Yada Pruksachatkun; Kai-Wei Chang; Rahul Gupta
short_summary: Benchmarks open-ended generation bias using prompts across profession, gender, race, religion, and political ideology with toxicity and sentiment-style metrics.
research_relevance: Good fit if your migration work asks how models continue prompts about immigrant or refugee groups.

title: RealToxicityPrompts: Evaluating Neural Toxic Degeneration in Language Models
authors: Samuel Gehman; Suchin Gururangan; Maarten Sap; Yejin Choi; Noah A. Smith
short_summary: Uses naturally occurring web prompts to measure toxic continuations from language models.
research_relevance: Useful for studying whether migration-related prompts trigger toxic or dehumanizing continuations.

title: HONEST: Measuring Hurtful Sentence Completion in Language Models
authors: Debora Nozza; Federico Bianchi; Dirk Hovy
short_summary: Measures hurtful completions for demographic prompts and supports multilingual evaluation.
research_relevance: Methodologically useful for migration because hurtfulness can be evaluated in multilingual and identity-specific prompts.

title: I'm sorry to hear that: Finding New Biases in Language Models with a Holistic Descriptor Dataset
authors: Eric M. Smith; Melissa Hall; Melanie Kambadur; Eleonora Presani; Adina Williams
short_summary: Introduces HolisticBias, a broad descriptor dataset for probing many identity axes and intersectional descriptions.
research_relevance: Strong source for expanding beyond standard protected attributes into descriptors likely relevant to migrants.

title: UNQOVERing Stereotyping Biases via Underspecified Questions
authors: Tao Li; Daniel Khashabi; Tushar Khot; Ashish Sabharwal; Vivek Srikumar
short_summary: Uses underspecified questions to expose stereotyped associations in model answers.
research_relevance: Very relevant for decision prompts where evidence is missing, vague, or underspecified.

title: Marked Personas: Using Natural Language Prompts to Measure Stereotypes in Language Models
authors: Myra Cheng; Esin Durmus; Dan Jurafsky
short_summary: Prompts models to generate personas for marked and unmarked groups, then identifies distinguishing stereotype terms.
research_relevance: Good method for studying othering, exoticization, and flattening in immigrant/refugee personas.

title: Evaluating and Mitigating Discrimination in Language Model Decisions
authors: Alex Tamkin; Amanda Askell; Liane Lovitt; Esin Durmus; Nicholas Joseph; Shauna Kravec; Karina Nguyen; Jared Kaplan; Deep Ganguli
short_summary: Audits discrimination in language-model decisions and explores mitigation strategies.
research_relevance: Directly useful if your migration project includes admission, eligibility, triage, hiring, housing, or welfare-style decisions.

title: AI generates covertly racist decisions about people based on their dialect
authors: Valentin Hofmann; Pratyusha Kalluri; Dan Jurafsky; Sharese King
short_summary: Shows that dialect cues can trigger covertly racist model decisions even when overt stereotypes are less visible.
research_relevance: Important warning for migration work because accent, dialect, and non-native writing can act as proxy identity signals.

title: Large language models that replace human participants can harmfully misportray and flatten identity groups
authors: Angelina Wang; Jamie Morgenstern; John P. Dickerson
short_summary: Studies how LLMs used as stand-ins for people can misrepresent and homogenize identity groups.
research_relevance: Relevant to any method that asks LLMs to simulate migrants, refugees, policymakers, or public opinion.

title: Bias and Fairness in Large Language Models: A Survey
authors: Isabel O. Gallegos; Ryan A. Rossi; Joe Barrow; Md Mehrab Tanjim; Sungchul Kim; Franck Dernoncourt; Tong Yu; Ruiyi Zhang; Nesreen K. Ahmed
short_summary: Surveys bias types, evaluation settings, mitigation methods, and fairness concerns in LLMs.
research_relevance: Best starting overview for positioning your project and finding related benchmark families.

title: On the Dangers of Stochastic Parrots
authors: Emily M. Bender; Timnit Gebru; Angelina McMillan-Major; Shmargaret Shmitchell
short_summary: A foundational critique of large language models, emphasizing scale, documentation, environmental costs, and representational harms.
research_relevance: Use for theoretical framing, especially around whose language and social realities are represented in training data.

title: Quite Good, but Not Enough: Nationality Bias in Large Language Models - a Case Study of ChatGPT
authors: Shucheng Zhu; Weikang Wang; Ying Liu
short_summary: Evaluates nationality bias in ChatGPT across countries, prompt types, temperatures, and Chinese/English outputs.
research_relevance: Directly relevant to migration because nationality often functions as a proxy for migrant identity and deservingness.

title: CIVICS: Building a Dataset for Examining Culturally-Informed Values in Large Language Models
authors: Giada Pistilli; Alina Leidinger; Yacine Jernite; Atoosa Kasirzadeh; Alexandra Sasha Luccioni; Margaret Mitchell
short_summary: Introduces a multilingual value-sensitive prompt dataset including immigration, welfare, LGBTQI rights, disability rights, and surrogacy.
research_relevance: Very relevant for studying immigration as a value-laden topic across languages and cultures.

title: PapersPlease: A Benchmark for Evaluating Motivational Values of Large Language Models Based on ERG Theory
authors: Junho Myung; Yeongje Park; Sunwoo Kim; Shin Yoo; Alice Oh
short_summary: Builds immigration-inspector moral dilemmas to test how LLMs prioritize human needs and respond to identity cues.
research_relevance: A direct benchmark model for migration decision scenarios and denial/approval bias.

title: Digital Gatekeepers: Exploring Large Language Model's Role in Immigration Decisions
authors: Yicheng Mao; Yang Zhao
short_summary: Studies LLMs in immigration decision support through choice experiments and interviews, reporting nationality-related stereotypes and privilege effects.
research_relevance: Directly aligned with migration, administrative fairness, and decision-making bias.

title: Learning the Topic, Not the Language: How LLMs Classify Online Immigration Discourse Across Languages
authors: Andrea Nasuto; Stefano M. Iacus; Francisco Rowe; Devika Jain
short_summary: Fine-tunes LLMs to classify immigration discourse across languages and analyzes language coverage and pretraining bias.
research_relevance: Relevant if your IR group studies retrieval/classification of migration discourse or multilingual dataset construction.

title: A Perspectival Mirror of the Elephant: Investigating Language Bias on Google, ChatGPT, YouTube, and Wikipedia
authors: Queenie Luo; Michael Puett; Michael D. Smith
short_summary: Compares how major information systems reflect language and cultural perspectives, including English-centric framing.
research_relevance: Useful conceptual bridge between IR, search systems, LLMs, and multilingual migration narratives.

title: The Woman Worked as a Babysitter: On Biases in Language Generation
authors: Emily Sheng; Kai-Wei Chang; Prem Natarajan; Nanyun Peng
short_summary: Early controlled-prompt study showing that neural generation varies by demographic group and can produce biased sentiment or regard.
research_relevance: Useful ancestor for open-ended migrant/refugee prompt continuation studies.

title: Social Bias Frames: Reasoning about Social and Power Implications of Language
authors: Maarten Sap; Saadia Gabriel; Lianhui Qin; Dan Jurafsky; Noah A. Smith; Yejin Choi
short_summary: Introduces a framework and dataset for explaining implied social bias, power dynamics, and harmful implications in text.
research_relevance: Good bridge from benchmark scores to interpretable explanations of anti-migrant or xenophobic language.

title: The Risk of Racial Bias in Hate Speech Detection
authors: Maarten Sap; Dallas Card; Saadia Gabriel; Yejin Choi; Noah A. Smith
short_summary: Shows that hate-speech detectors can over-associate African American English and identity markers with toxicity.
research_relevance: Important caution for migration discourse classifiers because identity dialects and group terms can become false toxicity signals.

title: ToxiGen: A Large-Scale Machine-Generated Dataset for Adversarial and Implicit Hate Speech Detection
authors: Thomas Hartvigsen; Saadia Gabriel; Hamid Palangi; Maarten Sap; Dipankar Ray; Ece Kamar
short_summary: Creates a dataset for implicit and adversarial hate speech toward minority groups using machine generation and human validation.
research_relevance: Useful for studying subtle anti-immigrant hate that may not use explicit slurs.

title: Semantics derived automatically from language corpora contain human-like biases
authors: Aylin Caliskan; Joanna J. Bryson; Arvind Narayanan
short_summary: Adapts implicit-association testing to word embeddings, showing that distributional semantics encode social stereotypes.
research_relevance: Foundational for understanding why stereotype tests often trace back to corpus co-occurrence patterns.

title: Man is to Computer Programmer as Woman is to Homemaker? Debiasing Word Embeddings
authors: Tolga Bolukbasi; Kai-Wei Chang; James Zou; Venkatesh Saligrama; Adam Tauman Kalai
short_summary: Classic paper on identifying and reducing gender directions in word embeddings.
research_relevance: Useful historical context for mitigation, but also a reminder that debiasing one axis may miss deeper representational structure.

title: Lipstick on a Pig: Debiasing Methods Cover up Systematic Gender Biases in Word Embeddings But do not Remove Them
authors: Hila Gonen; Yoav Goldberg
short_summary: Argues that common embedding debiasing methods hide rather than remove gendered structure.
research_relevance: Useful skepticism for migration-bias mitigation claims that only remove explicit identity terms.

title: Gender Bias in Coreference Resolution: Evaluation and Debiasing Methods
authors: Jieyu Zhao; Tianlu Wang; Mark Yatskar; Vicente Ordonez; Kai-Wei Chang
short_summary: Introduces WinoBias-style evaluation for gender bias in coreference systems.
research_relevance: Good example of constructing controlled templates where stereotypes compete with linguistic evidence.

title: Data Statements for Natural Language Processing: Toward Mitigating System Bias and Enabling Better Science
authors: Emily M. Bender; Batya Friedman
short_summary: Proposes structured documentation of language datasets, including speaker, annotator, and language-variety information.
research_relevance: Very relevant for documenting migration datasets, especially language variety, geography, and speaker community.

title: Datasheets for Datasets
authors: Timnit Gebru; Jamie Morgenstern; Briana Vecchione; Jennifer Wortman Vaughan; Hanna Wallach; Hal Daume III; Kate Crawford
short_summary: Proposes dataset documentation to improve accountability, provenance, and responsible reuse.
research_relevance: Useful for building a migration benchmark that future users can audit and interpret.

title: Model Cards for Model Reporting
authors: Margaret Mitchell; Simone Wu; Andrew Zaldivar; Parker Barnes; Lucy Vasserman; Ben Hutchinson; Elena Spitzer; Inioluwa Deborah Raji; Timnit Gebru
short_summary: Introduces structured reporting for model performance, intended use, limitations, and ethical considerations.
research_relevance: Useful template for reporting migration-bias audits across model versions and deployment settings.

title: Holistic Evaluation of Language Models
authors: Percy Liang; Rishi Bommasani; Tong Lee; Dimitris Tsipras; Dilara Soylu; Michihiro Yasunaga; Yian Zhang; Deepak Narayanan; Yuhuai Wu; Ananya Kumar; Benjamin Newman; Binhang Yuan; Bobby Yan; Ce Zhang; Christian Cosgrove; Christopher Manning; Christopher Re; Diana Acosta-Navas; Drew Hudson; Eric Zelikman; Esin Durmus; Faisal Ladhak; Frieda Rong; Hong-Yu Ren; Huaxiu Yao; Jue Wang; Keshav Santhanam; Laurel Orr; Lucia Zheng; Mert Yuksekgonul; Mirac Suzgun; Nathan Kim; Neel Guha; Niladri Chatterji; Omar Khattab; Peter Henderson; Qian Huang; Ryan Chi; Sang Michael Xie; Shibani Santurkar; Surya Ganguli; Tatsunori Hashimoto; Thomas Icard; Tianyi Zhang; Vishrav Chaudhary; William Yang Wang; Xuechen Li; Yifan Mai; Yuhui Zhang; Yuta Koreeda
short_summary: Introduces HELM, a broad evaluation framework for language models across scenarios and metrics including bias and toxicity.
research_relevance: Useful model for designing a multi-metric migration-bias evaluation rather than one benchmark score.

title: DecodingTrust: A Comprehensive Assessment of Trustworthiness in GPT Models
authors: Boxin Wang; Weixin Chen; Hengzhi Pei; Chulin Xie; Mintong Kang; Chenhui Zhang; Chejian Xu; Zidi Xiong; Ritik Dutta; Rylan Schaeffer; Sang Truong; Simran Arora; Mantas Mazeika; Dan Hendrycks; Zinan Lin; Yu Cheng; Sanmi Koyejo; Dawn Song; Bo Li
short_summary: Evaluates GPT models across trustworthiness dimensions including toxicity, stereotypes, robustness, privacy, and ethics.
research_relevance: Useful for thinking about bias as one part of a broader trust and risk evaluation suite.

title: Red Teaming Language Models to Reduce Harms: Methods, Scaling Behaviors, and Lessons Learned
authors: Deep Ganguli; Liane Lovitt; Jackson Kernion; Amanda Askell; Yuntao Bai; Saurav Kadavath; Ben Mann; Ethan Perez; Nicholas Schiefer; Kamal Ndousse; Andy Jones; Sam Bowman; Anna Chen; Tom Conerly; Nova DasSarma; Dawn Drain; Nelson Elhage; Sheer El-Showk; Stanislav Fort; Zac Hatfield Dodds; Tom Henighan; Danny Hernandez; Tristan Hume; Josh Jacobson; Scott Johnston; Shauna Kravec; Catherine Olsson; Sam Ringer; Eli Tran-Johnson; Dario Amodei; Tom Brown; Nicholas Joseph; Sam McCandlish; Chris Olah; Jared Kaplan; Jack Clark
short_summary: Studies red-teaming methods for surfacing harmful model behavior and scaling those methods.
research_relevance: Useful if you want adversarial prompts around border control, asylum, nationality, religion, or legal status.

title: Whose Opinions Do Language Models Reflect?
authors: Shibani Santurkar; Esin Durmus; Faisal Ladhak; Cinoo Lee; Percy Liang; Tatsunori Hashimoto
short_summary: Compares model responses to public-opinion distributions across demographic groups, showing whose views are poorly represented.
research_relevance: Useful for immigration because migration policy questions are value-laden and group opinions differ systematically.

title: Global Voices, Local Biases: Socio-Cultural Prejudices across Languages
authors: Anjishnu Mukherjee; Chahat Raj; Ziwei Zhu; Antonios Anastasopoulos
short_summary: Scales association-style bias evaluation across 24 languages with culturally situated bias dimensions.
research_relevance: Strong fit for migration work that should not assume English or Western stereotype dimensions.

title: FA*IR: A Fair Top-k Ranking Algorithm
authors: Meike Zehlike; Francesco Bonchi; Carlos Castillo; Sara Hajian; Mohamed Megahed; Ricardo Baeza-Yates
short_summary: Defines fairness constraints for top-k rankings so protected groups are not underrepresented in ranked prefixes.
research_relevance: Core IR fairness paper for thinking about exposure of migrant-related sources or communities in ranked results.

title: Fairness of Exposure in Rankings
authors: Ashudeep Singh; Thorsten Joachims
short_summary: Formalizes exposure fairness in rankings and connects position bias with group opportunity.
research_relevance: Highly relevant for retrieval/reranking audits where source visibility is part of harm.

title: Fairness in Ranking: A Survey
authors: Meike Zehlike; Ke Yang; Julia Stoyanovich
short_summary: Surveys fairness definitions, interventions, and evaluation strategies for ranking systems.
research_relevance: Good orientation paper for translating LLM-bias questions into IR ranking terminology.

title: Auditing Search Engines for Differential Satisfaction Across Demographics
authors: Rishabh Mehrotra; Ashton Anderson; Fernando Diaz; Amit Sharma; Hanna Wallach; Emine Yilmaz
short_summary: Proposes an internal audit framework for measuring whether search engines underserve demographic groups.
research_relevance: Useful design pattern for auditing LLM-assisted search across migrant communities, languages, or information needs.

title: The Silicon Ceiling: Auditing GPT's Race and Gender Biases in Hiring
authors: Lena Armstrong; Abbey Liu; Stephen MacNeil; Danae Metaxa
short_summary: Audits GPT in hiring-related resume scoring and generation, finding race and gender biases and immigrant-marker effects.
research_relevance: Useful adjacent HCI/audit study because immigrant markers emerge even when the nominal task is hiring rather than migration.

title: A model of often mixed stereotype content: Competence and warmth respectively follow from perceived status and competition
authors: Susan T. Fiske; Amy J. C. Cuddy; Peter Glick; Jun Xu
venue: Journal of Personality and Social Psychology
short_summary: Introduces the Stereotype Content Model, organizing group stereotypes around warmth and competence as functions of status and competition.
research_relevance: Very useful for operationalizing migrant stereotypes beyond positive/negative sentiment.

title: The BIAS map: Behaviors from intergroup affect and stereotypes
authors: Amy J. C. Cuddy; Susan T. Fiske; Peter Glick
venue: Journal of Personality and Social Psychology
short_summary: Links warmth/competence stereotypes to emotional reactions and behavioral tendencies such as helping, neglect, or harm.
research_relevance: Useful bridge from stereotype representation to predicted treatment or decision behavior.

title: Dehumanization: An Integrative Review
authors: Nick Haslam
short_summary: Reviews animalistic and mechanistic dehumanization and related psychological mechanisms.
research_relevance: Critical for migration discourse where people are framed as floods, invasions, burdens, or contaminants.

title: Uncertainty, Threat, and the Role of the Media in Promoting the Dehumanization of Immigrants and Refugees
authors: Victoria M. Esses; Stelian Medianu; Andrea Lawson
short_summary: Connects uncertainty, threat, media framing, and dehumanization of immigrants and refugees.
research_relevance: Directly relevant to LLM-generated or retrieved migration narratives that amplify threat frames.

title: Not an outgroup, not yet an ingroup: Immigrants in the Stereotype Content Model
authors: Tiane L. Lee; Susan T. Fiske
short_summary: Applies the Stereotype Content Model to immigrant groups, showing differentiated stereotypes across immigrant subgroups.
research_relevance: Essential for avoiding a single generic migrant category in benchmark design.

title: Intergroup Competition and Attitudes Toward Immigrants and Immigration: An Instrumental Model of Group Conflict
authors: Victoria M. Esses; Lynne M. Jackson; Tamara L. Armstrong
short_summary: Models anti-immigrant attitudes through perceived competition and group conflict.
research_relevance: Useful theoretical basis for threat, scarcity, jobs, welfare, and burden frames.

title: Public Attitudes Toward Immigration
authors: Jens Hainmueller; Daniel J. Hopkins
short_summary: Reviews political-science evidence on immigration attitudes, including economic, cultural, and psychological explanations.
research_relevance: Best high-level social-science overview for migration attitudes.

title: The Hidden American Immigration Consensus: A Conjoint Analysis of Attitudes toward Immigrants
authors: Jens Hainmueller; Daniel J. Hopkins
short_summary: Uses conjoint experiments to estimate how immigrant attributes affect public preferences.
research_relevance: Very useful design inspiration for LLM decision audits that vary migrant attributes.

title: What Triggers Public Opposition to Immigration? Anxiety, Group Cues, and Immigration Threat
authors: Ted Brader; Nicholas A. Valentino; Elizabeth Suhay
short_summary: Shows how emotional anxiety, group cues, and threat information can increase opposition to immigration.
research_relevance: Useful for separating economic threat, cultural threat, and affective triggers in prompts.

title: Predisposing Factors and Situational Triggers: Exclusionary Reactions to Immigrant Minorities
authors: Paul M. Sniderman; Louk Hagendoorn; Markus Prior
short_summary: Studies how predispositions and situational cues interact to produce exclusionary reactions toward immigrant minorities.
research_relevance: Relevant for prompt experiments where context cues activate latent stereotypes.

title: Politicized Places: Explaining Where and When Immigrants Provoke Local Opposition
authors: Daniel J. Hopkins
short_summary: Explains local opposition to immigration through the interaction of demographic change and politicized national rhetoric.
research_relevance: Important for thinking about locality and context rather than treating migration attitudes as universal.

title: Framing: Toward Clarification of a Fractured Paradigm
authors: Robert M. Entman
short_summary: Classic definition of framing as selection and salience in communication.
research_relevance: Foundational for studying how LLMs select, emphasize, and summarize migration frames.

title: Framing as a Theory of Media Effects
authors: Dietram A. Scheufele
short_summary: Clarifies framing processes and distinguishes media frames from audience effects.
research_relevance: Useful for connecting generated text, retrieved sources, and downstream attitude effects.

title: How news content influences anti-immigration attitudes: Germany, 1993-2005
authors: Hajo G. Boomgaarden; Rens Vliegenthart
short_summary: Empirically links news content to anti-immigration attitudes over time.
research_relevance: Useful precedent for studying how repeated LLM exposure to frames could shape perceptions.

title: The European media discourse on immigration and its effects: a literature review
authors: Jakob-Moritz Eberl; Christine E. Meltzer; Tobias Heidenreich; Beatriz Herrero; Nora Theorin; Fabienne Lind; Rosa Berganza; Hajo G. Boomgaarden; Christian Schemer; Jesper Stromback
venue: Annals of the International Communication Association
short_summary: Reviews European immigration media discourse and its effects on attitudes and politics.
research_relevance: Excellent bridge between migration communication studies and LLM/IR frame analysis.

title: Legal Violence: Immigration Law and the Lives of Central American Immigrants
authors: Cecilia Menjivar; Leisy J. Abrego
short_summary: Develops the concept of legal violence to describe harmful effects of immigration law on everyday life.
research_relevance: Important for avoiding a narrow legal/administrative framing of migrant status.

title: Migrant 'Illegality' and Deportability in Everyday Life
authors: Nicholas De Genova
short_summary: Analyzes migrant illegality as socially and legally produced, with deportability shaping everyday life.
research_relevance: Essential for treating 'illegal immigrant' as a political/legal construction, not a neutral descriptor.

title: Race Prejudice as a Sense of Group Position
authors: Herbert Blumer
short_summary: Classic group-position theory of prejudice, emphasizing dominant-group claims over status and entitlement.
research_relevance: Useful for linking anti-migrant sentiment to perceived hierarchy, entitlement, and boundary maintenance.
